questions.q: Let $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2},\omega)$ be the splitting field of $x^3-2$ over $\mathbb{Q}$, where $\omega=e^{2\pi i/3}$. What is $[L:\mathbb{Q}]$?
questions.explain: The minimal polynomial of $\sqrt[3]{2}$ has degree $3$ and that of $\omega$ has degree $2$, so $[L:\mathbb{Q}]=3\cdot 2=6$ by the tower law.

questions.q: What is the order of $\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)/\mathbb{Q})$, where $\zeta_5=e^{2\pi i/5}$?
questions.explain: The fifth cyclotomic polynomial $\Phi_5$ has degree $\varphi(5)=4$, so the Galois group has order $4$ (it is cyclic, isomorphic to $(\mathbb{Z}/5)^\times$).

questions.q: The nontrivial automorphism of $\mathbb{Q}(i)/\mathbb{Q}$ sends $i\mapsto -i$. What is its image on $2+3i$, written as $a+bi$?
questions.explain: Complex conjugation fixes $\mathbb{Q}$ and sends $i\mapsto -i$, so $2+3i\mapsto 2-3i$.

questions.q: The Galois correspondence for a Galois extension $L/K$ matches intermediate fields $K\subseteq F\subseteq L$ with:
questions.explain: The correspondence $F\leftrightarrow \operatorname{Gal}(L/F)$ is an inclusion-reversing bijection: bigger fields correspond to smaller subgroups.

questions.q: $L=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})$ has $\operatorname{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q})\cong(\mathbb{Z}/2)^2$ of order $4$. How many intermediate fields strictly between $\mathbb{Q}$ and $L$ are there?
questions.explain: The Klein four-group has three proper nontrivial subgroups, each of order $2$, corresponding to the three quadratic subfields $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2}),\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{3}),\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{6})$.

questions.q: Under the Galois correspondence, an intermediate field $F$ is a normal extension of the base $K$ iff:
questions.explain: Normality in the tower matches normal subgroups in the group: $F/K$ is Galois iff $\operatorname{Gal}(L/F)\trianglelefteq \operatorname{Gal}(L/K)$, and then $\operatorname{Gal}(F/K)\cong \operatorname{Gal}(L/K)/\operatorname{Gal}(L/F)$.

questions.q: A polynomial $f\in\mathbb{Q}[x]$ is solvable by radicals iff:
choices: its Galois group is solvable (has an abelian tower)
questions.explain: Galois' theorem: $f$ is solvable by radicals iff $\operatorname{Gal}(f/\mathbb{Q})$ is a solvable group — i.e., admits a subnormal series with abelian quotients.

questions.q: What is $|A_5|$, the order of the alternating group that obstructs solving the general quintic?
questions.explain: $|A_5|=5!/2=60$. Since $A_5$ is simple and non-abelian, $S_5$ is not solvable, so the general quintic has no radical formula.

questions.q: Which polynomial's Galois group over $\mathbb{Q}$ is all of $S_5$, preventing a radical solution?
questions.explain: $x^5-x-1$ is irreducible over $\mathbb{Q}$, has exactly two non-real roots (so complex conjugation is a transposition), and a $5$-cycle from transitivity — these generate $S_5$.

$\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt[3]{2})$ contains only one root of $x^3-2$; the other two roots $\omega\sqrt[3]{2},\omega^2\sqrt[3]{2}$ are complex. A normal extension must contain all roots of the minimal polynomial of any element.

questions.q: For a finite Galois extension $L/K$ one has $|\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)|=[L:K]$. If $[L:K]=12$, what is $|\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)|$?
questions.explain: Galois is exactly the equality $|\operatorname{Gal}(L/K)|=[L:K]$. If either side is $12$, so is the other.

questions.q: Which statement characterizes separability of $\alpha$ over $K$?
choices: the minimal polynomial $m_\alpha$ has integer coefficients
the minimal polynomial $m_\alpha$ has distinct roots in an algebraic closure
$\alpha$ has finite order in the multiplicative group $L^\times$
$\alpha$ lies in a tower of quadratic extensions
questions.explain: By definition $\alpha$ is separable over $K$ iff its minimal polynomial over $K$ has no repeated roots in any extension. In characteristic zero this is automatic.

questions.q: The primitive element theorem says that every finite separable extension $L/K$ is:
choices: simple — generated by a single element $\theta$ with $L=K(\theta)$
questions.explain: A finite separable extension admits a primitive element: $L=K(\theta)$ for some $\theta\in L$. Such an extension is called simple.

questions.q: The element $\theta=\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{3}$ is primitive for $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})/\mathbb{Q}$. What is the degree of its minimal polynomial over $\mathbb{Q}$?
questions.explain: $[\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3}):\mathbb{Q}]=4$, and if $\theta$ is primitive then $[\mathbb{Q}(\theta):\mathbb{Q}]=4$, so $m_\theta$ has degree $4$ (explicitly $x^4-10x^2+1$).

questions.q: Where can the primitive element theorem fail?
choices: for inseparable extensions in positive characteristic, e.g. $\mathbb{F}_p(x,y)/\mathbb{F}_p(x^p,y^p)$
questions.explain: Separability is essential. $\mathbb{F}_p(x,y)/\mathbb{F}_p(x^p,y^p)$ has degree $p^2$ but every element generates a subfield of degree at most $p$, so it is not simple.
